Got a call from the supervisor at 3am saying that he was unable to get the Spartan profile that we set up to open correctly. Stayed with him on phone and head out to location. Told him that it sounded like line, cable head, slips or just another bad ccl. Got out there as he was running in the well and everything was working. He got to the marker joint and lost ccl again as he was pulling up to tie in. I started to troubleshoot and did not find anything. Was able to show them that the problem was down hole by disconnecting the slips and getting collars to show up by using their multimeter. XConnect was called because the engineer was receiving a short after gun 8. He was able to set the plug and shoot gun 1 before the switch showed a short. The engineer was able to read the switch with the surface test box through the slip rings, so the surface panels were swapped out to make sure there was not a short at the panel. The issue persisted and the decision was made to skip gun 2 through 7 and shoot guns 8 through 11.

Once on surface the shot guns were removed and the tool string verified again with the verifier box. The switch stem for gun 7 was removed from the tool string and when tested with the verifier box independently it showed “yes” for a detonator which indicated a short.
While troubleshooting the gun system the SQM noticed a lot of moisture on the bulkhead of gun 7, on other switch stems in the trailer, as well as in the sub cart used when breaking down guns. The concern about excessive moisture was communicated with the crew and they understood saying they had been cleaning the switch stems before installing them on new guns.

The switch stem was returned to the XConnect facility and further evidence of moisture and corrosion was discovered. 
The moisture was able to travel into the switch molding and reach the switch board creating corrosion that eventually caused the stem to short out.

Arrived on location to troubleshoot three RAIL guns that were not reading on surface. Broke down and inspected each associated carrier and tandem sub. Identified one carrier that would read in but not out. The switch was replaced, and the guns were reassembled. All switches then read correctly both individually and in line.

It is unclear what troubleshooting steps the day crew attempted prior to my arrival. Only one of the three barrels had an actual issue, which suggests the guns may have been set aside as soon as they failed to read.

The failed switch has been pulled and will be taken back to the shop for further review.

*GO Civitas – XC*
 *Update on DC Insert Breakage*
This is a follow-up on the earlier reports of DC Inserts breaking. I visited location to better understand what the crews were seeing and to observe both the installation and cleanup process. They mentioned that *eight inserts had broken within the past 36 hours*, all involving the same two DCs and the same two retainer nuts that have been in service for some time.
I grabbed a recently rebuilt DC tremoved its retainer nut and insert. The Retainer Nut in my opinion was heavily torqued. After disassembly, I found the insert had also cracked.

*Initial Measurements*

*Retainer Nut Measurements*
(_See the attached Print Screenshot_)
• A. Internal Depth: 4.014”
• B. Overall Length: 4.125”
• C. Top of Thread to Main Shoulder: 0.627”
• D. Main Body Length: 3.498”
*Insert Measurements (XBH500-203)*
*13 samples* were measured at the site, and *13 samples* at the GO Facility.

*On-Site Samples:*
• 2 brand new inserts measured at *4.597” and 4.598”*
• 1 previously shaved-down insert, likely from the earlier Gen 1 retainer nut issue
• 10 used or broken inserts measured between *4.587” and 4.592”* (after compression)
*GO Facility Samples:*
• 3 shaved-down inserts measured between *4.567”–4.573”*
• 10 new inserts measured consistently at *4.597”*
*Key Observations*
• All three retainer nuts (two on-site, one at the facility) showed signs of wear and potential over-torquing, specifically, flared outer shoulders and worn internal shoulders.
• Retainer nut main body lengths were slightly below spec:
    ◦ Facility sample: *3.495”*
    ◦ On-site: *3.497”–3.498”*
• Insert lengths were a bit over tolerance:
    ◦ Print spec: *4.575”  Tolerances are set at ± 0.015”*
    ◦ Several inserts measured around *4.597”*, putting them *0.007”–0.008” over* the upper limit of the Tolerance. .020-.023 over Print Diagram.
*Summary*
The combination of slightly oversized inserts and worn retainer nuts appears to the cause of these Inserts cracking

Stage 7 after attempting to set the plug the supervisor was prompted with a short circuit after Gun 8. He thought the plug was set, upon POOH it was realized that it was not, he got tricked on the shot plot not realizing the subtle differences of a direct short or igniter firing. Tested the gunstring on surface and confirmed a short after gun 8. Broke down guns 7 & 8. Tested communication through stem 8. Performed a successful insulation test on BEP of gun 8 and TEP of gun 7, as well as the BEP of gun 7. Performed a successful insulation test of stems 7 and 8 as well. Surface Tested stem 7 without success, overcurrent detected every time with no ability to inventory. Which is interesting that it happens to pass an insulation test now, just will not inventory.  I'm leaning towards the board frying prior to Gate sequence A opening. I'll keep digging into the stem. Also to note, although should still last but this stem either has 10 runs, or 13. No one knew.

SM/Caliber called last night around 12:30AM. They set the plug and then the system detected an open circuit at gun 1. Went through as many things as we could to troubleshoot the issue, including pulling up to the first shot depth. Could not move much or pump on the tool string due to ball in place. Also wanted to stay on depth for the remaining shots. Engineer rolled enough rate to see a pressure increase to establish that the plug did set. So the decision was made by the SM company rep to skip gun 1 and shoot the remaining 9 guns. ASF. SM called it a stage. 
    Met the Caliber FSM on location to troubleshoot the issue. All of the wiring and internals of the gun looked good. Boxed the gun and found that the combo switch would not register the FF0E connection. Disconnected it from the gun and got the same result. Will take the combo switch in question back to the shop for further inspection.

The FSM for Caliber/SM called around 10:30pm and said they were pulling out of the well due to not being able to read past gun 2 at the marker joint.  Asked if was an open or short but he did not know. Got to location at 11:50pm and they were already out of the well. Checked the shot log and the gun string was showing an open. We broke the gun string down and found that the sub on gun 2 was missing the bow spring around the pin of the insert. This was the nut down side. Also to note that this gun had been sent back to the shop at some point for a defect and sent back out. This can be seen in the pictures below.

The initial two runs (White) and (RED) wells were successful. However, subsequent runs encountered some issued. NON XC related

CE 2 (WHITE)
Ran in with Full Toolstring; stacked out at ~5,800 ft.
Removed the plug and attempted again, but stacked out at the same depth.
Frac pumped for a sweep but lost all pressure – indicating a hole in the casing.
CE 1 (RED)
Ran in with Full Toolstring; stacked out at ~5,500 ft and became stuck.
Attempted multiple recovery methods:
Flowed the well back
Surged the well
Water hammered
Pumped 60 bbl/m
Set the plug
None were successful. The toolstring finally came free after threatening to initiate the BRT.
Formation material was found in the setting sleeve.
Ran in with guns and gauge ring:
Tagged the plug 200 ft lower than the setting depth.
Pumped on it but saw almost no pressure increase – confirming a hole in the casing.
CE 4 (GREEN)
Ran in with guns and gauge ring; stacked out at ~8,500 ft.
Attempted pumping to move past but was unsuccessful – indicating another hole in the casing.

-CE 3 (BLUE). Made a successful run

•	Initiated pumping to the preset plug depth and commenced igniter firing. 
•	At 22:00, encountered an acceptable interruption in current flow; however, observed no indication of the plug's setting. 
•	A subsequent switch scan revealed the firing of gun one, prompting the Bottom Hole Assembly (BHA) to be POOH for investigative measures.

Upon arrival at the designated location at 0500 hours, the night shift supervisor was instructing the day supervisor regarding the operation of the software and the functionalities of the XFire system. The day supervisor possessed a basic understanding of the system's operation.
Run One
The first gun run was initiated, and a switch scan was performed at this time. Additional scans were made at tie-in and prior to pumpdown. The supervisor pulled into plug depth and scanned the switches to set the plug. A positive plug set was indicated. The supervisor commenced shooting the first zone, but he was unsure of the scan/firing procedure; I instructed the supervisor during the shooting phase. The first gun was shot stationary due to the short spacing from the plug to the first shot. Then, the 10-gun string ASF/POOH was shot.
During the plug set operation, my focus was on the software issue that we encountered the previous day*(Tier 1 OK). I was checking the two windows to confirm the plug set.
Note*: During the plug switch scan, the Company Man expressed dissatisfaction with the performance of the XFire system from his experience the previous day.
Run Two
Gun run two was RIH, and a switch scan was performed by the supervisor. Additional scans were made at tie-in and prior to pumpdown. The supervisor pulled into plug depth and scanned the switches to set the plug. A positive plug set was indicated. The first gun was shot stationary due to the short spacing from the plug to the first shot. During the firing sequence of gun two, the supervisor scanned the switches again. He fired gun two before completing the scan sequence, from the scan it appeared to fire. He Continued to shoot the remaining gun string; he called all eleven shots as fired off his shot sheet.
I reviewed the switch scans to confirm gun two fired; I visually inventoried the fired switches and found gun eleven was not fired. The gun was confirmed un- fired on the surface. The supervisor and I met with the Company Man to review the situation with the unfired gun. We did not delve into details on the cause of the failure at this time. The Company Man replied that "he can change out equipment."
Note: During run two, I noticed that the supervisor was rescanning switches after each shot. I assumed there was an issue with the auto-scan. I called Eric and discussed the issue, and he said it was a procedural error by the supervisor. This was an extra unneeded scan. I instructed the supervisor to eliminate the extra scan.




Run Three
Prior to the start of run three, I was informed that the Company Man requested Hunting switches be used on the remaining job. A hotshot was initiated, and switches were on location at ~1300 hours. Gun run two was RIH, and a switch scan was performed by the supervisor. Additional scans were made at tie-in and prior to pumpdown. The supervisor pulled into plug depth and scanned the switches to set the plug. A positive plug set was indicated. ASF/POOH.
A Tier 1 FSM arrived to help transition to the Titan system. I removed the XFire system and helped install the Hunting panel. I reviewed with the crew the potential issue of having two switch systems on location and the possibility of the wrong switch being installed. All XFire switches were isolated from the loading area. During testing of the switches with the Hunting panel, switches could not be read. I asked the FSM if he changed the BRT XFire switch to a Hunting switch. A Hunting switch was installed in the BRT. All switch communications evaluated good.

Day engineer said he wished the system grayed the whole selection out or marked all the way across after gun was fired instead of just saying fired in small letters. Day shift also shot a stage at end of shift when I was there and clicked yes after setting the plug and the plug stayed highlighted in blue and he armed it and was fixing to shoot it again and I told home to stop because for some reason he was attempting to set the plug again and the system did not move to gun 1. We had to highlight and select gun 1 then arm it as if we were skipping. I went back and looked at the log and sent it to Hasan. He did click Yes that all indications of plug set. What I think happened and Hasan agrees is when the message box popped up to confirm plug set they it was in line with the plug and maybe he double clicked the mouse and it closed the box and cursor was on screen in the right place to highlight the plug again. The next time we shoot we are going to drag that confirm box away from any selections to prevent that from being a chance to happen.
